The stock was then projected ten years forward under a constant catch held at the terminal year's level, propagating parameter uncertainty through both the MVN and MCMC approaches (@sec-projections). The two approaches agreed closely at the projection-start state, and the projection intervals widened only gradually because 'opakapaka is long-lived and matures slowly, so the existing adult biomass declines nearly deterministically under constant catch while future recruitment and selectivity uncertainty accumulate over the projection years (@fig-paka-projection).

## 2020 western and central Pacific Ocean bigeye tuna {#sec-case-bet}

The second case study used the data from a fleets-as-areas model described in the appendix of the 2020 assessment of bigeye tuna (*Thunnus obesus*) in the western and central Pacific Ocean [WCPO\;@ducharme-barth_stock_2020]. The bundled data object contained the biological parameters, catch and standardized CPUE observations, length and weight composition data, configured at a quarterly time step across the fisheries defined in the fleets-as-areas assessment, with selectivity specified by fishery as either logistic or double-normal forms.

The diagnostic configuration used the quarterly standardized CPUE series as four seasonal indices, fitted length compositions from fisheries 8--14, and fitted weight compositions from fisheries 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, and 15. Sensitivity runs indicated that the size-composition likelihoods pulled the absolute spawning-biomass scale below the SS3 and MFCL reference models, while posterior-predictive composition checks indicated uneven relative weighting among fisheries. The retained fit therefore used fishery-specific variance adjustments to the length-frequency (LF) and weight-frequency (WF) data sets. Weight compositions for fishery 5 remained inactive after diagnostic runs failed to converge cleanly. Recruitment deviations were estimated across the model period, catchability was estimated separately by quarterly index, and selected selectivity parameters were estimated for the fisheries with active composition data. Growth, natural mortality, steepness, recruitment variability, effort creep (assumed to be zero), and the remaining selectivity parameters were fixed at the bundled reference values. The quarterly catch history used by the model is shown by fishery and catch-unit group in @fig-bet-catch-weight and @fig-bet-catch-numbers.

The final bigeye tuna (BET) model configuration converged according to `nlminb`, with a KKT-style[^1] projected gradient close to zero after accounting for active parameter bounds; the larger raw gradient is reported separately in @tbl-bet-fit-summary. Remaining active selectivity bounds are reported in @tbl-bet-selectivity-issues. Fitting this diagnostic configuration from the retained-cache starting values to the maximum-likelihood estimate took approximately `r bet_mle_runtime_text` on the workstation used to build this document; the final optimizer restart recorded `r bet_mle_iterations` iterations, `r bet_mle_function_evals` function evaluations, and `r bet_mle_gradient_evals` gradient evaluations. Hessian-based uncertainty was computed with `RTMB::sdreport()` using a supplied fixed-effect Hessian from `optimHess()` with a finite-difference step of $10^{-4}$; this Hessian was positive definite, with minimum eigenvalue `r signif(bet$diagnostics$biomass_uncertainty_min_hessian_eigenvalue, 3)`. The additive objective decomposition was still dominated numerically by the downweighted composition likelihoods, especially the weight-composition component, while the CPUE component contributed the abundance trend information and the recruitment-deviation density regularized quarterly departures from the stock-recruitment curve. The estimated unfished spawning biomass was approximately `r format(round(bet$report$B0), big.mark = ",")` mt, close to the MFCL implied scale of `r format(round(bet_mfcl_b0), big.mark = ",")` mt and closer to the SS3 fixed-selectivity scale of `r format(round(bet_ss3_fix_b0), big.mark = ",")` mt than an earlier higher-composition-weight diagnostic fit. The final-year relative spawning biomass was `r round(bet_final_sb_b0, 3)` when scaled by $SB_0$ (@tbl-bet-depletion). The dynamic depletion ratio was slightly lower (`r round(bet_final_dyn_dep, 3)`) because it uses the time-varying unfished spawning biomass denominator rather than the static $SB_0$ denominator.

[^1]: Following the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions for bound-constrained optimization, gradient components at an active bound are not treated as evidence of non-convergence provided their sign is consistent with the constraint (e.g., pushing further against, rather than away from, the bound).

An additional exploratory BET model was fitted after aggregating the 15 original fisheries to six fisheries using the grouped downweighted LF/WF configuration described above (@tbl-bet-agg6-grouping). This sensitivity was intended to test whether the many small fisheries were materially affecting the abundance scale or the CPUE fit, rather than to replace the retained diagnostic model. Under the six-fishery aggregation, $B_0$ was `r format(round(bet_agg6_metrics$B0), big.mark = ",")` mt and final-year $SB/SB_0$ was `r round(bet_agg6_final_sb_b0, 3)`, compared with `r format(round(bet$report$B0), big.mark = ",")` mt and `r round(bet_final_sb_b0, 3)` for the retained 15-fishery model (@tbl-bet-agg6-summary). The CPUE log-RMSE was also similar (`r sprintf("%.3f", bet_agg6_metrics$log_cpue_rmse)` for the six-fishery model versus `r sprintf("%.3f", bet_cpue_log_rmse)` for the retained model), and the six-fishery CPUE and biomass trajectories are included in @fig-bet-cpue-fit and @fig-bet-biomass. The six-fishery selectivity curves in @fig-bet-agg6-selectivity should be interpreted as pooled selectivities for the aggregation groups rather than direct replacements for the original fishery-specific curves.

Diagnostic plots in the `opal` BET vignette compare observed and predicted CPUE by seasonal index, observed and predicted length and weight compositions, fitted recruitment deviations, and estimated selectivity-at-length and selectivity-at-age curves. The CPUE fit in @fig-bet-cpue-fit tracked the broad decline in standardized abundance from the early 1950s through the low-abundance period in the 2000s, while allowing the four quarterly indices to retain their own scale and observation uncertainty; simulated CPUE series from the fitted model showed the amount of variation expected under the fitted lognormal observation model. Additionally, illustrative fits to the same index from MFCL and SS3 models are also shown for reference. The CPUE OSA residuals in @fig-bet-osa-residuals were centred close to zero and fell within approximately $\pm 2$ residual units, but the Q-Q panels were clearly under-dispersed: standard deviation of normalised residuals (SDNRs) were below one for each index month and for all indices combined (@tbl-bet-cpue-sdnr). For the independent lognormal CPUE likelihood these OSA residuals are equivalent to standardized log residuals, so the flattened Q-Q pattern indicated that the fitted CPUE observation variance was larger than the residual variation, rather than a small number of large outliers [@dunn_randomized_1996; @thygesen_validation_2017]. The length- and weight-composition diagnostics in @fig-bet-lf-diagnostics and @fig-bet-wf-diagnostics sum variance-adjusted pseudo-counts over all years and independently simulate fitted row-level compositions before pooling those simulated counts within fishery. The corresponding grouped diagnostics for the six-fishery sensitivity are shown together in @fig-bet-agg6-composition-diagnostics using the same row-level pooling logic. To show the unaggregated temporal structure, @fig-bet-lf12-year-composition and @fig-bet-wf15-year-composition give year-specific examples for length-composition fishery 12 and weight-composition fishery 15. Example LF and WF composition OSA residual diagnostics are shown for fishery 12 and fishery 15 in @fig-bet-lf-composition-residuals and @fig-bet-wf-composition-residuals; these two panels are examples rather than the full residual set. The by-fishery Q-Q diagnostics in @fig-bet-composition-residual-qq summarise all active composition residuals against a fixed standard-normal reference line, rather than a fitted Q-Q line, so below-one dispersion remains visible; @fig-bet-agg6-composition-residual-qq repeats this Q-Q layout for the six-fishery grouped sensitivity using the same `compResidual::resMulti()` residual calculation on the grouped compositions. Following the diagnostic summaries commonly applied in CCSBT assessments of southern bluefin tuna, @tbl-bet-composition-sdnr reports finite bin-level OSA SDNRs generated with `r bet_comp_residual_source` for both the retained 15-fishery model and the matched six-fishery sensitivity. The near-one OSA SDNRs are conditional on the variance-adjusted input sample sizes used in the retained likelihood; the mean input $N$ by retained-model fishery ranges from `r signif(bet_comp_mean_input_n_range[1], 3)` to `r signif(bet_comp_mean_input_n_range[2], 3)` after downweighting. Because these variance adjustments produce fractional pseudo-counts, including sub-unit effective sample sizes for some rows, the multinomial composition likelihood in this diagnostic fit should be interpreted as a weighted pseudo-likelihood convention rather than a literal integer-count multinomial sampling model. The close Q-Q alignment is therefore a check of the randomized bin-level residual distribution for the weighted pseudo-data likelihood, not evidence that the raw composition data are perfectly fitted.

The CPUE residual pattern supported reducing the fixed CPUE $\tau$ (additional observation standard deviation) and/or scaling the CPUE SEs down. The retained BET fits fixed $\tau$ at `r bet_cpue_fixed_tau_text` for all four CPUE indices; the only active CPUE parameters were the four catchability terms. Relative to the earlier cached run with $\tau = 0.1$, setting $\tau$ near zero reduced the mean CPUE standard deviation from 0.222 to 0.198, but increased the all-index CPUE SDNR only from 0.513 to `r round(bet$diagnostics$cpue_sdnr$SDNR[bet$diagnostics$cpue_sdnr$index == "All indices"], 3)`. Removing the fixed $\tau$ therefore helped only modestly and did not resolve the under-dispersion by itself. Further CPUE upweighting would require scaling the CPUE SEs down and should not be treated as an automatic tuning to SDNR = 1 without checking the consequences to the biomass estimates and composition fits.

The selectivity diagnostics in @tbl-bet-selectivity-issues and @fig-bet-selectivity-length show where the fitted `opal` selectivity curves differ from both the MFCL and SS3 reference curves and which selectivity terms remain estimated in the final constrained map. Several weakly identified width terms were fixed at their SS3-initialized values to obtain stable Hessian-based uncertainty; @tbl-bet-selectivity-issues lists these fixed terms explicitly. The broader estimated-parameter blocks and their optimizer bounds are summarized in @tbl-bet-parameter-bounds. The recruitment deviations in @fig-bet-recruitment-deviations show the seasonal pattern of estimated process variation. The biomass and depletion trajectories in @fig-bet-biomass show the long-term decline in spawning biomass ($SB$), static ($SB/SB_0$), and dynamic depletion ($SB_t/SB_{t,F=0}$) for the retained and six-fishery `opal` models relative to MFCL and SS3 models fitted to the same data set. The retained `opal` uncertainty ribbon is the approximate 95% interval from the positive-definite supplied-Hessian `sdreport()` calculation.

The `opal` spawning-biomass scale in @fig-bet-biomass in the retained downweighted fit was much closer to the SS3 and MFCL reference trajectories than initial model runs. However, a review of the document cache and the `opal` BET scripts suggests several relevant differences from the *equivalent* SS3 and MFCL models that could lead to differences in the `opal` estimated biomass scale. First, the double-normal `opal` selectivity curves are length-based (as opposed to age-based cubic splines in MFCL) and are converted through the PLA; the bundled double-normal width starts require conversion from a legacy SS3 scale to the current width scale, and diagnostic runs in the `opal` repository showed that biomass scale is sensitive to this selectivity scaling and map. Second, the fit used a simplified harvest-fraction removal calculation applied at the start of each timestep, no weight composition for fishery 5, and an intentionally downweighted size-composition likelihood. These differences can all change the vulnerable biomass implied by the same catch and CPUE data, so the absolute $SB$ comparison is best viewed as a diagnostic cross-platform check rather than a tuned stock-status result. As with the 'opakapaka example, these results are intended to demonstrate the end-to-end `opal` workflow as a proof-of-concept and do not reflect a carefully considered stock assessment model intended for management advice.

# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

`opal` provides an age-structured, length-based stock assessment framework implemented in RTMB, with a modular internal structure, support for multiple data types and likelihood forms, random-effects estimation, catch-based projections, Bayesian inference, and a set of built-in diagnostics. The two case studies demonstrate its application to fit length composition, weight composition, and index data in single-species assessments in both a simulated data context, where the truth is known, and with a real data tuna example. Both examples serve to highlight the successful proof-of-concept of the `opal` framework in its ability to adequately estimate population dynamics and easily provide high-quality diagnostic outputs for model evaluation and validation.

The continued development of case-studies is central to the development of `opal`, with case studies serving multiple purposes: they provide a basis for testing and validating the model, they demonstrate the model's capabilities to the broader community, and they provide a starting point for new users to learn how to use the software. Additionally, new modules are added to the code base to meet the needs of the case studies as they arise. The integrated testing approach ensures that new modules do not break existing functionality, while safely providing a pathway to grow model functionality. Use of simulated data in case studies provides an objective basis for evaluating `opal`'s performance, and for cross-platform comparisons with existing software.

One of the primary motivations for the development of `opal` was to enable the calculation of more recently developed statistical diagnostics that are either unavailable or challenging to calculate in existing software platforms such as SS3 or MFCL. The included case-studies serve to showcase and demonstrate some of these diagnostics that are readily calculated from an `opal` model fit. In both cases, the `check_estimability` function can be used to rapidly identify if the model has converged and if all parameters are identifiable. In the case of the bigeye tuna case study this led to the identification of a number of poorly determined selectivity parameters, which could subsequently be held fixed, and also prompted an investigation of a model with a simplified fisheries structure where all selectivity parameters were estimable. The 'opakapaka case study also demonstrated the use of the `simulate` function to generate posterior predictive distributions for the observed data in order to ascertain whether the model was able to adequately capture the observed data. Additionally, the bigeye tuna case study demonstrated the use of OSA residuals to evaluate the fit of the model to the observed data, and to identify potential issues with the model fit. The OSA residuals can be implicitly calculated as a by-product of the underlying RTMB framework, and are therefore readily available for any model fit.

The other driving motivation for `opal` development was to facilitate the application and implementation of Bayesian inference within integrated assessment models. Bayesian inference provides a more holistic characterization of uncertainty in model estimates through the incorporation of prior information through parameter priors. This can have the additional benefit of stabilizing model estimates, as well as to allow the estimation of parameters that may otherwise be poorly determined. Doing so may allow analysts to move away from grids of alternative fixed parameters model runs to a more simplified model ensemble where uncertainty in key parameters is represented by their prior. Lastly, having a Bayesian stock assessment modelling framework readily available to analysts can formalize the inclusion of information from precursor, external analyses[^2] into the stock assessment model.

[^2]: For example, the development of movement or population scale priors from external analyses, such as SEAPODYM [@lehodey_spatial_2008] or the DTU fine-scale movement model [@magnusson_scoping_2025], in a potential future spatially-explicit version of `opal`.

As mentioned previously, one of the benefits of the case-study driven approach is the ability to conduct cross-platform comparisons with existing and well-established stock assessment software. In the 'opakapaka case study, the `opal` model was able to be closely configured to match the existing SS3 operating and estimation models. Though there are slight differences in the underlying population dynamics calculations[^3], the `opal` model was able to closely track the SS3 spawning biomass trajectory which demonstrates the viability of the `opal` population dynamics structure. Given that we had access to the 'known-truth' from simulated data, this provided further confidence in the ability of `opal` to accurately estimate population dynamics. The bigeye tuna case study also demonstrated the ability of `opal` to closely track the spawning biomass and depletion trajectories from both SS3 and MFCL, though there were some differences in the absolute scale of the spawning biomass. These differences are likely due to differences in selectivity parameterization, composition weighting, and catch removal calculations between the three platforms. The case studies also highlighted some of the challenges associated with fitting complex integrated assessment models, particularly when using real data where the underlying truth is unknown. These challenges include issues with parameter identifiability, sensitivity to model configuration, and the need for careful consideration of model diagnostics and outputs.

[^3]: `opal` applies harvest rates at the start of each time step, while SS3 applies a continuous total mortality at the mid-point of each time step. Additionally, all biological calculations in SS3 are based on the population demographics at the mid-point of the time step. 

While the case studies demonstrated `opal`'s ability to both successfully estimate population dynamics, and handle the data-volume and high-dimensionality of a real fisheries example[^4] they also identified numerous challenges, some still outstanding. Though the 'opakapaka case study demonstrated that `opal` was able to treat and estimate certain parameters as random effects, it resulted in a model that was substantially slower than the fixed effects model. It remains to be seen how this scales to higher dimensionality assessments as preliminary attempts to apply random effects in the bigeye tuna case study did not appear tractable[^5]. Similarly, while the `opal` model is now able to fairly rapidly converge to a solution in the highly dimensional bigeye tuna case study, this model begins optimization from fairly plausible initial values. Substantial time and effort went into developing both the plausible initial value set and the associated parameter bounds. Both SS3 and MFCL appear to more robustly converge from naive initial values so further work is warranted to improve the robustness of `opal`'s optimization. However, it is worth noting that both SS3 and MFCL estimate parameters in phases (MFCL has a fairly refined initialization sequence) which may contribute to their better optimization performance, while `opal` simultaneously estimates all parameters without phasing. Additionally, code optimization is still an active phase of development and several areas (e.g., length-weight conversion, selectivity parametrization, and size-composition) of the underlying `opal` model structure were re-factored during the development of the bigeye tuna case-study to improve the model performance. Further code optimization is likely needed to improve the speed and robustness of the model, particularly for high-dimensional assessments.

In addition to the aforementioned issues, the real-world data that formed the basis for the bigeye tuna case study illustrated several further challenges. The initial richer selectivity maps were not fully estimable: several double-normal width and right-tail parameters were weakly identified, some ran to bounds, and Hessian-based uncertainty was not available until the $\log(B_0)$ lower bound was relaxed and selected weakly identified selectivity terms were fixed at their SS3-initialized values. The resulting model is therefore a constrained diagnostic fit rather than a fully tuned assessment. The case study also exposed practical issues that are common in integrated assessment models: the absolute biomass scale is sensitive to selectivity parameterization and composition weighting; the retained diagnostic fit used fishery-specific LF/WF variance adjustments to balance biomass scale and posterior-predictive composition variability; weight-composition fishery 5 was excluded after convergence diagnostics were poor; and the mixed catch units and quarterly time-step encoding require careful checks when comparing against SS3 and MFCL outputs. Finally, the size-composition residual diagnostics are computationally and statistically delicate because they operate on weighted pseudo-data likelihoods, so apparently good OSA residual behaviour must be interpreted alongside effective sample sizes, mean-composition diagnostics, and selectivity plausibility. This reinforces the need for continued development of both simulated and real-data case studies to ensure that the `opal` framework is robust and can be applied to a wide range of fisheries assessment scenarios.

[^4]: In terms of population dynamics structure the BET case study uses 15 fisheries, 268 time-steps, 40 quarterly age-classes, 95 length-bins, and 200 weight-bins. There are 4 quarterly CPUE indices, and 2 composition types (1,194 WF instances and 568 LF instances). The model is therefore a high-dimensional integrated assessment model that is representative of the complexity of real-world fisheries assessments.

[^5]: However, these attempts were made prior to certain code optimizations and reparametrizations in the selectivity and size-composition likelihood calculations, so it is possible that random effects estimation may be more tractable in future versions of `opal`.

Despite the progress to date, `opal` is far from a finished product. As `opal` transitions into the next phase of development multiple rich avenues for continued work exist across several core areas: documentation, development of the user interface, model testing, code optimization, research, and feature development. This work extends beyond simply coding a technically-sound population dynamics model, but also encompasses managing the user experience so that `opal` is an accessible, easy to learn tool that does what is needed. Continued success will depend on both engagement and investment from the broader fisheries assessment community beyond the existing development team. Additionally, a focused and prioritized list of requirements is needed to inform the development pathway and to ensure that the resulting software meets the needs of the community. In the interim, several extensions are planned based on either existing feature lists [@punt_essential_2020] or the development team's experiences with `opal` to date. Areas identified for ongoing work include:

- refinement of existing case studies; 
- development of additional case studies (e.g., Indian Ocean yellowfin tuna fleets-as-areas case study based on simulated data);
- code optimization (particularly of the selectivity and size-composition likelihood calculations);
- derivation of biological and fishery reference points;
- additional likelihood components (e.g., age, tag or close-kin mark-recapture data);
- alternative selectivity forms;
- and additional population dimensionality (e.g., spatial structure, sex, or length).

Within the context of the WCPFC, `opal` could be applied to the upcoming assessment of north Pacific Ocean blue shark (*Prionace glauca*), or in a research capacity to the assessment of south Pacific albacore (*Thunnus alalunga*).

The work so far demonstrates the proof of concept of the R-based modular development and has made significant development steps for core model components. We suggest that continued development of `opal` provides an option for the core development stream for the next generation of tuna assessment software and can provide alternative model features and structures that would be suitable for assessments of various species (e.g., tuna, shark and billfish) within the WCPFC or other regional fishery management organizations.

# Acknowledgements {#sec-acknowledgements}

Development of opal is intended to be an open and collaborative effort, inclusive to the broader fisheries assessment community. To that end we are grateful to the many people that contributed to the project through informal discussions, and participation in periodic update meetings. While not an exhaustive list, this community includes: N. Davies (Te Takina Ltd), S. Hoyle (Hoyle Consulting), M. Maunder (Inter-American Tropical Tuna Commission), R. Bi (IATTC), F. Carvalho (PIFSC), G. Pilling (SPC), J. Hampton (SPC *emeritus*), and many others including participants in the 2025 CAPAM mini workshop on developing the next generation tuna stock assessment model as well as the 2026 SPC Pre-Assessment Workshop. Development of `opal` has received funding support from *WCPFC Project 123: Scoping the next generation of tuna stock assessment software*.

# Declaration of generative AI use {#sec-ai-declaration}

Generative AI tools were used to assist with code development, refactoring, and documentation, and with drafting and editing text in this document. All analytical choices, model specifications, diagnostic interpretations, and conclusions are those of the authors, who reviewed and verified all AI-assisted output.

# References {#sec-references}

::: {#refs}
:::

\newpage

# Tables {#sec-tables}

```{r}
#| label: tbl-paka-lf-convergence
#| tbl-cap: "`opal` 'opakapaka model metrics: positive-definite Hessian, maximum absolute gradient (mgc), minimum Hessian eigenvalue, parameter estimability, runtime, number of parameters, and sigma R."
#| echo: false

est <- paka$estim
est_re <- paka$estim_re

if (inherits(est, "condition")) {
  # check_estimability() failed and the condition was cached in Block B.
  min_eig_chr  <- "\u2014"
  estim_status <- paste0("check failed: ", conditionMessage(est))
} else {
  n_bad       <- length(est$WhichBad)
  min_eig_chr <- formatC(min(est$Eigen$values), format = "e", digits = 2)
  estim_status <- if (n_bad == 0) {
    "0 (all parameters estimable)"
  } else {
    bad_names <- rownames(est$Hess)[est$WhichBad]
    paste0(n_bad, " (", paste(bad_names, collapse = ", "), ")")
  }
}

if (inherits(est_re, "condition")) {
  # check_estimability() failed and the condition was cached in Block B.
  min_eig_chr_re  <- "\u2014"
  estim_status_re <- paste0("check failed: ", conditionMessage(est_re))
} else {
  n_bad_re       <- length(est_re$WhichBad)
  min_eig_chr_re <- formatC(min(est_re$Eigen$values), format = "e", digits = 2)
  estim_status_re <- if (n_bad_re == 0) {
    "0 (all parameters estimable)"
  } else {
    bad_names_re <- rownames(est_re$Hess)[est_re$WhichBad]
    paste0(n_bad_re, " (", paste(bad_names_re, collapse = ", "), ")")
  }
}

paka_nll = paka$report$lp_prior + paka$report$lp_penalty + paka$report$lp_rec + paka$report$lp_init_rec + sum(paka$report$lp_cpue) + sum(paka$report$lp_lf) + sum(paka$report$lp_wf)

paka_nll_re = paka$report_re$lp_prior + paka$report_re$lp_penalty + paka$report_re$lp_rec + paka$report_re$lp_init_rec + sum(paka$report_re$lp_cpue) + sum(paka$report_re$lp_lf) + sum(paka$report_re$lp_wf)

conv_tbl <- data.frame(
  Metric = c(
                 "Positive-definite Hessian",
                 "Max |gradient| (mgc)",
                 "Min Hessian eigenvalue",
                 "Inestimable parameters",
                 "Runtime (seconds)",
                 "N parameters (fixed)",
                 "N parameters (random)",
                 "sigma R"),
  Value_fe = c(as.character(paka$pdh),
            paka$mgc_sci,
            min_eig_chr,
            estim_status,
            round(as.numeric(paka$runtime, units = "secs"),digits=2),
            length(paka$par),
            0,
            paste0(round(exp(-0.6539265),digits=2), " (fixed)")),
  Value_re = c(as.character(paka$pdh_re),
            paka$mgc_sci_re,
            min_eig_chr_re,
            estim_status_re,
            round(as.numeric(paka$runtime_re, units = "secs"),digits=2),
            length(paka$par_re),
            length(paka$par)-(length(paka$par_re)-1),
            round(unname(exp(paka$par_re["log_sigma_r"])),digits=2)),
  check.names = FALSE
)
colnames(conv_tbl) <- c("Metric", "opal", "opal (RE)")

knitr::kable(conv_tbl, booktabs = TRUE, align = c("l", "l","l"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-paka-mcmc-diag
#| tbl-cap: "Posterior summaries and MCMC convergence diagnostics for the 'opakapaka opal model: the MLE estimate, posterior mean and standard deviation, 5th and 95th percentiles, bulk and tail effective sample sizes (ESS), and split-Rhat, for the scalar parameters (unfished spawning biomass and the two CPUE catchabilities) and three representative recruitment deviations."
#| echo: false

mdiag <- paka$mcmc_diag
knitr::kable(
  mdiag[, c("variable", "mle", "mean", "sd", "q5", "q95",
            "ess_bulk", "ess_tail", "rhat")],
  col.names = c("Parameter", "MLE", "Mean", "SD", "q5", "q95",
                "ESS bulk", "ESS tail", "Rhat"),
  digits = 3, booktabs = TRUE, align = c("l", rep("r", 8))
)
```

\newpage

```{r}
#| label: tbl-bet-fit-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Optimization and objective-component summary for the fitted WCPO bigeye tuna diagnostic model and matched six-fishery aggregation sensitivity. The Max KKT gradient is the maximum projected gradient after accounting for active parameter bounds. Objective components are a switch-based additive audit at the fitted parameter vector: the baseline has CPUE, length-composition (LF), and weight-composition (WF) likelihood switches off, and the CPUE, LF, and WF rows are single-switch differences from that baseline."
#| echo: false

format_bet_value <- function(x, digits = 2) {
  format(round(x, digits), big.mark = ",", nsmall = digits)
}

bet_fit_tbl <- data.frame(
  Metric = c(
    "nlminb convergence",
    "Objective",
    "Max |gradient|",
    "Max KKT gradient",
    "B0",
    "Baseline contribution",
    "CPUE contribution",
    "LF contribution",
    "WF contribution",
    "Objective-component total"
  ),
  `15-fishery BET` = c(
    bet$opt$convergence,
    format_bet_value(bet$opt$objective),
    round(bet$raw_grad, 4),
    round(bet$kkt_grad, 4),
    format(round(bet$report$B0), big.mark = ","),
    format_bet_value(bet_objective_components["Baseline contribution"]),
    format_bet_value(bet_objective_components["CPUE contribution"]),
    format_bet_value(bet_objective_components["LF contribution"]),
    format_bet_value(bet_objective_components["WF contribution"]),
    format_bet_value(sum(bet_objective_components))
  ),
  `6-fishery BET` = c(
    bet_agg6_metrics$convergence,
    format_bet_value(bet_agg6_metrics$nll),
    round(bet_agg6_metrics$raw_max_gradient, 4),
    round(bet_agg6_metrics$kkt_max_gradient, 4),
    format(round(bet_agg6_metrics$B0), big.mark = ","),
    format_bet_value(bet_agg6_objective_components["Baseline contribution"]),
    format_bet_value(bet_agg6_objective_components["CPUE contribution"]),
    format_bet_value(bet_agg6_objective_components["LF contribution"]),
    format_bet_value(bet_agg6_objective_components["WF contribution"]),
    format_bet_value(sum(bet_agg6_objective_components))
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)

knitr::kable(bet_fit_tbl, booktabs = TRUE, align = c("l", "r", "r"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-bet-depletion
#| tbl-cap: "Final-year relative spawning-biomass diagnostics for the fitted WCPO bigeye tuna diagnostic model."
#| echo: false

bet_depletion_tbl <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("SB/SB0", "SB/SB_F0"),
  Value = round(c(bet_final_sb_b0, bet_final_dyn_dep), 3),
  check.names = FALSE
)

knitr::kable(bet_depletion_tbl, booktabs = TRUE, align = c("l", "r"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-bet-cpue-sdnr
#| tbl-cap: "Standard deviation of normalized residuals (SDNR) and median absolute residual (MAR) for CPUE residuals in the fitted WCPO bigeye tuna diagnostic model and matched six-fishery aggregation sensitivity."
#| echo: false

bet_cpue_sdnr_tbl <- bind_rows(
  bet$diagnostics$cpue_sdnr %>% mutate(Model = "15F"),
  bet_agg6_cpue_sdnr %>% mutate(Model = "6F")
) %>%
  transmute(
    Model,
    `Index month` = index,
    n,
    SDNR = round(SDNR, 3),
    `95% LCI` = round(LCI, 3),
    `95% HCI` = round(HCI, 3),
    MAR = round(MAR, 3)
  ) %>%
  mutate(`Index month` = factor(
    `Index month`,
    levels = c("All indices", month.name[sort(unique(bet$data$cpue_data$month))])
  )) %>%
  arrange(`Index month`, Model) %>%
  mutate(`Index month` = as.character(`Index month`))

knitr::kable(bet_cpue_sdnr_tbl, booktabs = TRUE,
             align = c("l", "l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"))
```

\clearpage

```{r}
#| label: tbl-bet-composition-sdnr
#| tbl-cap: "Composition diagnostics by composition type and fishery for the fitted WCPO bigeye tuna diagnostic model and matched six-fishery aggregation sensitivity. OSA SDNR columns are finite bin-level `compResidual::resMulti()` residual summaries for both model configurations; the six-fishery residuals use grouped observed compositions and fitted grouped predictions from the raw sensitivity cache."
#| echo: false

fmt_tbl_num <- function(x, digits = 3) {
  ifelse(is.finite(x), format(round(x, digits), nsmall = digits), "--")
}
fmt_tbl_ci <- function(lci, hci, digits = 3) {
  ifelse(
    is.finite(lci) & is.finite(hci),
    sprintf(paste0("%.", digits, "f-%.", digits, "f"), lci, hci),
    "--"
  )
}

bet_comp_sdnr_tbl_15 <- bet_comp_diagnostic_summary %>%
  transmute(
    Model = "15F",
    Composition = composition,
    Fishery,
    Rows = n_rows,
    `OSA SDNR` = fmt_tbl_num(osa_sdnr),
    `OSA 95% CI` = fmt_tbl_ci(osa_sdnr_lci, osa_sdnr_hci),
    `Mean input N` = fmt_tbl_num(mean_input_n, 2),
    `Prop. RMSE` = fmt_tbl_num(prop_rmse, 4),
    `Mean size RMSE` = fmt_tbl_num(mean_size_rmse, 2),
    `Mean size bias` = fmt_tbl_num(mean_size_bias, 2)
  )

bet_comp_sdnr_tbl_6 <- bet_agg6_comp_fit_summary %>%
  transmute(
    Model = "6F",
    Composition = composition,
    Fishery,
    Rows = n_rows,
    `OSA SDNR` = fmt_tbl_num(osa_sdnr),
    `OSA 95% CI` = fmt_tbl_ci(osa_sdnr_lci, osa_sdnr_hci),
    `Mean input N` = fmt_tbl_num(mean_input_n, 2),
    `Prop. RMSE` = fmt_tbl_num(prop_rmse, 4),
    `Mean size RMSE` = fmt_tbl_num(mean_size_rmse, 2),
    `Mean size bias` = fmt_tbl_num(mean_size_bias, 2)
  )

bet_comp_sdnr_tbl <- bind_rows(bet_comp_sdnr_tbl_15, bet_comp_sdnr_tbl_6) %>%
  mutate(
    Composition = factor(Composition, levels = c("Length", "Weight")),
    Model = factor(Model, levels = c("15F", "6F"))
  ) %>%
  arrange(Composition, Model, suppressWarnings(as.integer(sub(".* ([0-9]+)$", "\\1", Fishery))))

bet_comp_sdnr_kable <- knitr::kable(
  bet_comp_sdnr_tbl,
  format = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "latex" else "html",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l", "l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r")
)
if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
  knitr::asis_output(paste0(
    "\\begingroup\\scriptsize\\setlength{\\tabcolsep}{4pt}\n",
    bet_comp_sdnr_kable,
    "\n\\endgroup"
  ))
} else {
  bet_comp_sdnr_kable
}
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-bet-selectivity-issues
#| tbl-cap: "Selectivity-parameter diagnostics by fishery for the fitted WCPO bigeye tuna diagnostic model. Estimated terms are estimated in the final constrained map; fixed terms were held at their SS3-initialized values. DN = double-normal. Bound flags identify any remaining estimated selectivity terms that are on parameter bounds."
#| echo: false

bet_sel_raw <- bet$diagnostics$selectivity_raw
sel_short_component <- c(
  "peak", "top", "asc. width", "desc. width", "initial sel.", "final sel."
)
bet_relevant_sel_cols <- function(sel_type) {
  if (sel_type == 1L) c(1L, 2L) else c(1L, 3L, 4L)
}
bet_sel_map <- bet_sel_raw$map_sel
bet_sel_pos <- which(names(bet_sel_raw$opt_par) == "par_sel")
bet_sel_level_pos <- setNames(bet_sel_pos, as.character(seq_along(bet_sel_pos)))

bet_sel_diag <- map_dfr(seq_len(nrow(bet_sel_map)), function(f) {
  map_dfr(seq_len(ncol(bet_sel_map)), function(p) {
    level <- as.character(bet_sel_map[f, p])
    if (is.na(level)) return(tibble())
    idx <- bet_sel_level_pos[[level]]
    estimate <- bet_sel_raw$opt_par[idx]
    lower <- bet_sel_raw$bounds$lower[idx]
    upper <- bet_sel_raw$bounds$upper[idx]
    bound_status <- case_when(
      is.finite(lower) && estimate <= lower + 1e-7 ~ "lower",
      is.finite(upper) && estimate >= upper - 1e-7 ~ "upper",
      TRUE ~ "free"
    )
    tibble(
      fishery = f,
      Form = if_else(bet_sel_raw$sel_type_f[f] == 1L, "logistic", "DN"),
      term = sel_short_component[p],
      bound_status = bound_status
    )
  })
})

bet_selectivity_issues_tbl <- map_dfr(
  sort(as.integer(names(bet_sel_raw$sel_specs))),
  function(f) {
    flags <- bet_sel_diag %>%
      filter(fishery == f, bound_status != "free")
    estimated_cols <- bet_sel_raw$sel_specs[[as.character(f)]]
    fixed_cols <- setdiff(bet_relevant_sel_cols(bet_sel_raw$sel_type_f[f]), estimated_cols)
    tibble(
      Fishery = f,
      Form = unique(bet_sel_diag$Form[bet_sel_diag$fishery == f]),
      `Est. terms` = paste(sel_short_component[estimated_cols], collapse = ", "),
      `Fixed terms` = if (length(fixed_cols) == 0L) {
        "None"
      } else {
        paste(sel_short_component[fixed_cols], collapse = ", ")
      },
      `Bound flag` = if (nrow(flags) == 0L) {
        "None"
      } else {
        paste(paste0(flags$term, " at ", flags$bound_status, " bound"),
              collapse = "; ")
      }
    )
  }
)

bet_selectivity_kable <- knitr::kable(
  bet_selectivity_issues_tbl,
  format = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "latex" else "html",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  longtable = FALSE,
  align = c("r", "l", "l", "l", "l")
)

bet_selectivity_kable
```
```{r}
#| label: tbl-bet-parameter-bounds
#| tbl-cap: "Estimated parameter blocks and optimizer bounds for the fitted WCPO bigeye tuna diagnostic model. Selectivity rows are summarized as a block because individual fishery terms are listed in the selectivity diagnostics table; recruitment deviations are summarized by their fitted range."
#| echo: false

format_bound_value <- function(x) {
  case_when(
    is.infinite(x) & x < 0 ~ "-Inf",
    is.infinite(x) & x > 0 ~ "Inf",
    TRUE ~ format(round(x, 3), nsmall = 3)
  )
}
format_estimate_range <- function(x) {
  paste(format(round(range(x, na.rm = TRUE), 3), nsmall = 3), collapse = " to ")
}
format_bound_range <- function(x) {
  finite_x <- x[is.finite(x)]
  if (!length(finite_x)) return("unbounded")
  paste(format(round(range(finite_x), 3), nsmall = 3), collapse = " to ")
}

bet_opt_par <- bet_sel_raw$opt_par
bet_bounds <- bet_sel_raw$bounds
bet_par_months <- month.name[sort(unique(bet$data$cpue_data$month))]
bet_logq_idx <- which(names(bet_opt_par) == "log_cpue_q")
bet_logb0_idx <- which(names(bet_opt_par) == "log_B0")
bet_parsel_idx <- which(names(bet_opt_par) == "par_sel")
bet_rdev_idx <- which(names(bet_opt_par) == "rdev_y")

bet_parameter_bounds_tbl <- bind_rows(
  tibble(
    Parameter = "log_B0",
    n = 1L,
    Estimate = paste0(
      format(round(bet_opt_par[bet_logb0_idx], 3), nsmall = 3),
      " (B0 = ", format(round(exp(bet_opt_par[bet_logb0_idx])), big.mark = ","), " mt)"
    ),
    Lower = format_bound_value(bet_bounds$lower[bet_logb0_idx]),
    Upper = format_bound_value(bet_bounds$upper[bet_logb0_idx])
  ),
  map_dfr(seq_along(bet_logq_idx), function(i) {
    idx <- bet_logq_idx[i]
    tibble(
      Parameter = paste0("log_cpue_q (", substr(bet_par_months[i], 1, 3), ")"),
      n = 1L,
      Estimate = format(round(bet_opt_par[idx], 3), nsmall = 3),
      Lower = format_bound_value(bet_bounds$lower[idx]),
      Upper = format_bound_value(bet_bounds$upper[idx])
    )
  }),
  tibble(
    Parameter = "par_sel",
    n = length(bet_parsel_idx),
    Estimate = format_estimate_range(bet_opt_par[bet_parsel_idx]),
    Lower = format_bound_range(bet_bounds$lower[bet_parsel_idx]),
    Upper = format_bound_range(bet_bounds$upper[bet_parsel_idx])
  ),
  tibble(
    Parameter = "rdev_y",
    n = length(bet_rdev_idx),
    Estimate = format_estimate_range(bet_opt_par[bet_rdev_idx]),
    Lower = format_bound_value(unique(bet_bounds$lower[bet_rdev_idx])),
    Upper = format_bound_value(unique(bet_bounds$upper[bet_rdev_idx]))
  )
)

bet_parameter_bounds_kable <- knitr::kable(
  bet_parameter_bounds_tbl,
  format = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "latex" else "html",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  longtable = FALSE,
  align = c("l", "r", "l", "l", "l")
)

bet_parameter_bounds_kable
```

\clearpage

```{r}
#| label: tbl-bet-agg6-grouping
#| tbl-cap: "Six-fishery aggregation used in the exploratory WCPO bigeye tuna BET sensitivity. Original fisheries were pooled only where catch units and selectivity form remained compatible. Catch percentages are calculated separately within metric-ton and thousands-of-fish catch units."
#| echo: false

bet_agg6_grouping_tbl <- bet_agg6$grouping %>%
  group_by(catch_units) %>%
  mutate(percent_within_units = 100 * total_catch / sum(total_catch)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  transmute(
    `Six-fishery group` = group,
    `Original fisheries` = paste0("F", gsub(",", ", F", old_fisheries)),
    `Catch units` = if_else(as.integer(catch_units) == 1L,
                            "metric tons", "thousands of fish"),
    `Selectivity form` = sel_type,
    `Within-unit catch (%)` = sprintf("%.1f", percent_within_units)
  )

knitr::kable(bet_agg6_grouping_tbl, booktabs = TRUE,
             align = c("r", "l", "l", "l", "r"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-bet-agg6-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Comparison of the retained 15-fishery WCPO bigeye tuna diagnostic model and the matched six-fishery aggregation sensitivity. Objective values are omitted because the composition data are aggregated differently and therefore are not directly comparable."
#| echo: false

fmt_int <- function(x) format(round(x), big.mark = ",")
bet_agg6_summary_tbl <- tibble(
  Model = c("15F", "6F"),
  Fisheries = c(15L, 6L),
  `Base LF/WF downweight` = paste0(
    c(bet_lfwf_downweight_mult, bet_agg6_lfwf_downweight_mult), "x"
  ),
  `B0 (mt)` = c(fmt_int(bet$report$B0), fmt_int(bet_agg6_metrics$B0)),
  `Final SB/SB0` = sprintf("%.3f", c(bet_final_sb_b0, bet_agg6_final_sb_b0)),
  `Final SB/SB_F0` = sprintf("%.3f", c(bet_final_dyn_dep, bet_agg6_final_dyn_dep)),
  `CPUE log-RMSE` = sprintf("%.3f", c(bet_cpue_log_rmse, bet_agg6_metrics$log_cpue_rmse)),
  `Max KKT gradient` = sprintf("%.4f", c(bet$kkt_grad, bet_agg6_metrics$kkt_max_gradient))
)

knitr::kable(bet_agg6_summary_tbl, booktabs = TRUE,
             align = c("l", rep("r", 7)))
```


\newpage

# Figures {#sec-figures}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-paka-lf-biomass
#| fig-cap: "Estimated spawning-biomass trajectory for the 'opakapaka `opal` model estimated using either fixed effects or random effects (RE) compared with the SS3 operating model (OM) and estimation model (EM), 1949--2023."
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| fig-width: 8
calendar_years = paka$ssb$year
paka_opal_fe = data.frame(model = rep("opal", length(calendar_years)),
                          year = calendar_years,
                          value = paka$sdreport$value[which(names(paka$sdreport$value) == "spawning_biomass_y")][1:length(calendar_years)],
                          se = paka$sdreport$sd[which(names(paka$sdreport$value) == "spawning_biomass_y")][1:length(calendar_years)])
paka_opal_fe$lo95 = paka_opal_fe$value - 1.96 * paka_opal_fe$se
paka_opal_fe$hi95 = paka_opal_fe$value + 1.96 * paka_opal_fe$se

paka_opal_re = data.frame(model = rep("opal (RE)", length(calendar_years)),
                          year = calendar_years,
                          value = paka$sdreport_re$value[which(names(paka$sdreport_re$value) == "spawning_biomass_y")][1:length(calendar_years)],
                          se = paka$sdreport_re$sd[which(names(paka$sdreport_re$value) == "spawning_biomass_y")][1:length(calendar_years)])
paka_opal_re$lo95 = paka_opal_re$value - 1.96 * paka_opal_re$se
paka_opal_re$hi95 = paka_opal_re$value + 1.96 * paka_opal_re$se

ybmax_lf <- max(paka$ssb$estimate, paka$om$SSB, paka$em$SSB, na.rm = TRUE)

ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = paka_opal_fe,
              aes(year, ymin = lo95, ymax = hi95, fill = model), alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_ribbon(data = paka_opal_re,
              aes(year, ymin = lo95, ymax = hi95, fill = model), alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_line(data = paka_opal_fe,
            aes(x = year, y = value, colour = "opal"),
            linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(data = paka_opal_re,
            aes(x = year, y = value, colour = "opal (RE)"),
            linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(data = paka$om,
            aes(x = Yr, y = SSB, colour = "SS3 OM"),
            linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = paka$em,
            aes(x = Yr, y = SSB, colour = "SS3 EM"), linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_colour_opal("Model",breaks = c("opal", "opal (RE)", "SS3 OM", "SS3 EM")) +
  scale_fill_opal("Model", breaks = c("opal", "opal (RE)", "SS3 OM", "SS3 EM")) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Spawning biomass", colour = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ybmax_lf * 1.2)) +
  theme_opal()
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-paka-cpue-ppc
#| fig-cap: "Posterior predictive checks using the `bayesplot` package for the 'opakapaka commercial CPUE index (fishery 1) under the opal model. Simulated replicates in aqua, observations in dark teal. Left to right: replicate trajectories, density overlay, and probability integral transform (PIT) empirical CDF (ECDF) calibration check."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 7
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

nsims <- 50

  mle_sims = as.data.frame(paka$sims_cpue[which(opaka_data$cpue_data$fishery==1),]) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "sim", values_to = "cpue") %>%
  filter(sim %in% sample(unique(sim), nsims))
  mle_sims$x = sort(rep(opaka_data$cpue_data$year[which(opaka_data$cpue_data$fishery==1)],nsims))


# --- opal palette wiring -----------------------------------------------------
# bayesplot's 6 scheme slots run light -> dark:
#   light / light_highlight  -> yrep replicate lines & PIT bands
#   dark / dark_highlight     -> the observed y curve / ECDF line
opal_sim <- "#7DD2DB"   # simulated replicates (aqua)
opal_obs <- "#0E4049"   # observed (dark teal, emphasis)

color_scheme_set(c(
  opal_sim,   # light
  "#5EBFCB",  # light_highlight
  "#27A7BE",  # mid  (opal teal)
  "#1F8B9E",  # mid_highlight
  "#155F6B",  # dark
  opal_obs    # dark_highlight
))

# --- data --------------------------------------------------------------------
idx <- which(opaka_data$cpue_data$fishery == 1)

y    <- log(opaka_data$cpue_data$value[idx])         # observed, log units
yrep <- t(as.matrix(paka$sims_cpue[idx, ]))          # [draws x obs]

# guard against NA columns (years without CPUE) that break density estimation
ok   <- stats::complete.cases(t(yrep)) & !is.na(y)
y    <- y[ok]
yrep <- yrep[, ok, drop = FALSE]
stopifnot(ncol(yrep) == length(y))

set.seed(1)
draws <- sample(nrow(yrep), min(nsims, nrow(yrep)))

# --- panels ------------------------------------------------------------------
p_dens <- ppc_dens_overlay(y, yrep[draws, , drop = FALSE]) +
  ggplot2::ggtitle("CPUE density overlay") +
  xlab("log(CPUE)") +
  ylab("Density") +
  theme_opal()

p_pit <- ppc_pit_ecdf(y, yrep) +
  ggplot2::ggtitle("PIT ECDF") +
  theme_opal()

p_traj <- ggplot() +
  ylab("log(CPUE)") +
  xlab("Year") +
  geom_line(data = mle_sims, aes(x = x, y = cpue, group = sim),
            colour = opal_sim, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_line(data = opaka_data$cpue_data[idx, ],
            aes(x = year, y = log(value)),
            colour = opal_obs, linewidth = 1.5, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  ggtitle("CPUE trajectories") +
  theme_opal()

p_traj + p_dens + p_pit +
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-paka-mcmc-trace
#| fig-cap: "MCMC traces for the scalar parameters of the 'opakapaka opal (with LF) model --- unfished spawning biomass and the two CPUE catchabilities, on the log scale --- across eight chains (colours). Navy dashed lines mark the MLE. Overlapping, stationary chains indicate good mixing."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 8

ggplot(paka$mcmc_trace, aes(iter, value, colour = chain)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(data = paka$mcmc_mle_ref, aes(yintercept = mle),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = opal_cols["navy"], linewidth = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ param, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = opal_chains) +
  labs(x = "Post-warmup iteration", y = "Value", colour = "Chain") +
  theme_opal()
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-paka-projection
#| fig-cap: "'Opakapaka spawning biomass: historical uncertainty and a 10-year constant-catch projection (200 draws per method); the last 60 years are shown. Teal solid = estimated MLE; grey dashed = SS3 operating model; navy = SS3 estimation model. Coloured ribbons give the 50% (darker) and 90% (lighter) intervals for the MVN and MCMC approaches, and the amber line is the MCMC historical median. The vertical dashed line marks the end of the historical period (2024)."
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| fig-width: 8

ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = paka$proj_hist_ribbon,
              aes(year, ymin = lo90, ymax = hi90, fill = method), alpha = 0.12) +
  geom_ribbon(data = paka$proj_hist_ribbon,
              aes(year, ymin = lo50, ymax = hi50, fill = method), alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_line(data = subset(paka$proj_hist_ribbon, method == "MCMC"),
            aes(year, med, colour = method), linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_line(data = paka$proj_hist_mle, aes(year, sbio),
            colour = opal_proj["MVN"], linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_line(data = paka$proj_om, aes(Yr, SSB),
            colour = opal_cols["truth"], linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = paka$proj_em, aes(Yr, SSB),
            colour = opal_cols["navy"], linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2024, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_ribbon(data = paka$proj_ribbon,
              aes(year, ymin = lo90, ymax = hi90, fill = method), alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_ribbon(data = paka$proj_ribbon,
              aes(year, ymin = lo50, ymax = hi50, fill = method), alpha = 0.30) +
  geom_line(data = paka$proj_ribbon, aes(year, med, colour = method),
            linewidth = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = opal_proj) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = opal_proj) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Spawning biomass", colour = "Method", fill = "Method") +
  theme_opal()
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: prep-bet-catch-by-fishery

bet_catch_by_fishery <- bet$diagnostics$catch_by_fishery
if (is.null(bet_catch_by_fishery)) {
  stop("BET catch-by-fishery diagnostics are not available in the cached results")
}

bet_catch_by_fishery <- bet_catch_by_fishery %>%
  mutate(
    fishery_num = as.integer(fishery),
    Fishery = factor(paste("Fishery", fishery_num),
                     levels = paste("Fishery", sort(unique(fishery_num))))
  )
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-bet-catch-weight
#| fig-cap: "Observed quarterly catch for WCPO bigeye tuna fisheries with catch recorded in metric tons. Facets use a common y-axis scale across the weight-catch fisheries."
#| fig-height: 4.8
#| fig-width: 8

ggplot(filter(bet_catch_by_fishery, units == "weight"),
       aes(x = decimal_year, y = catch)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.18, fill = opal_cols["teal"], alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ Fishery, ncol = 4, scales = "fixed") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::breaks_pretty(n = 4)) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_comma(),
    limits = c(0, NA),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Catch (metric tons)") +
  theme_opal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 6),
    strip.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-bet-catch-numbers
#| fig-cap: "Observed quarterly catch for WCPO bigeye tuna fisheries with catch recorded in thousands of fish. Facets use a common y-axis scale across the numbers-catch fisheries."
#| fig-height: 4.8
#| fig-width: 8

ggplot(filter(bet_catch_by_fishery, units == "numbers"),
       aes(x = decimal_year, y = catch)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.18, fill = opal_cols["teal"], alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ Fishery, ncol = 4, scales = "fixed") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::breaks_pretty(n = 4)) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_comma(),
    limits = c(0, NA),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Catch (thousands of fish)") +
  theme_opal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 6),
    strip.text = element_text(size = 7)
  )
```


```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-bet-cpue-fit
#| fig-cap: "Observed, predicted, and simulated standardized CPUE for the fitted `opal` WCPO bigeye tuna diagnostic model, shown by index month. Vertical error bars show one fitted standard deviation on the log scale; light teal lines show fitted-model simulations from the retained 15-fishery model. The matched six-fishery aggregation sensitivity and illustrative fits by MFCL and SS3 are also shown. The RMSE of the log-residuals is shown for each model and index month."
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| fig-width: 8

bet_cpue <- bet$data$cpue_data %>%
  mutate(
    index = factor(month,
                   levels = sort(unique(month)),
                   labels = month.name[sort(unique(month))]),
    pred = bet$report$cpue_pred,
    sigma = bet$report$cpue_sigma,
    lower = exp(log(value) - sigma),
    upper = exp(log(value) + sigma)
  )

bet_cpue_sim <- bet$report$cpue_sim %>%
  mutate(
    index = factor(month,
                   levels = sort(unique(month)),
                   labels = month.name[sort(unique(month))]),
    simulated = exp(simulated_log)
  )

# load xmodel
bet_xmodel_cpue <- readRDS(file.path("cached", "bet", "wcpo_bet_xmodel_cpue.rds"))

# lookup table to translate ts -> year/month/index (same for all models)
ts_lookup <- bet_cpue %>%
  distinct(ts, year, month, index)

xmodel_cpue_long <- bet_xmodel_cpue %>%
  filter(id %in% c("mfcl-v11", "02-fix-sel")) %>%
  mutate(model = recode(id, "mfcl-v11" = "MFCL", "02-fix-sel" = "SS3")) %>%
  left_join(ts_lookup, by = c("Time" = "ts")) %>%
  transmute(
    year, month, index,
    pred  = Exp,
    value = Obs,
    sigma = SE,
    lower = exp(log(value) - sigma),
    upper = exp(log(value) + sigma),
    model
  )

bet_cpue$model <- "opal"

bet_agg6_cpue_long <- bet_agg6$cpue %>%
  transmute(
    year, month,
    index = factor(index_month, levels = levels(bet_cpue$index)),
    pred,
    value,
    sigma = log(upper) - log(value),
    lower,
    upper,
    model = "6-fishery opal"
  )

bet_cpue_combined <- bind_rows(bet_cpue, bet_agg6_cpue_long, xmodel_cpue_long)

# calculate goodness-of-fit metrics for each model and index
cpue_gof <- bet_cpue_combined %>%
  mutate(log_resid = log(value) - log(pred)) %>%
  group_by(index, model) %>%
  summarise(
    n     = n(),
    rmse  = sqrt(mean(log_resid^2)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(index, rmse) %>%
  select(index, model, rmse) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = model, values_from = rmse)

# long-format RMSE labels for plotting (one row per index/model)
cpue_gof_labels <- bet_cpue_combined %>%
  mutate(log_resid = log(value) - log(pred)) %>%
  group_by(index, model) %>%
  summarise(rmse = sqrt(mean(log_resid^2)), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(label = paste0(model, ": ", sprintf("%.3f", rmse))) %>%
  left_join(
    tibble(
      model = c("opal", "6-fishery opal", "SS3", "MFCL"),
      vjust = c(1.5, 3.0, 4.5, 6.0)
    ),
    by = "model"
  )

ggplot(bet_cpue_combined, aes(x = year)) +
  geom_line(data = bet_cpue_sim,
            aes(y = simulated, group = sim_id),
            colour = opal_role["simulated"], alpha = 0.5, linewidth = 0.32) +
  geom_errorbar(data = filter(bet_cpue_combined, model == "opal"),
                aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.35,
                colour = "grey20", alpha = 0.85, linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(data = filter(bet_cpue_combined, model == "opal"),
             aes(y = value), shape = 21, size = 1.25,
             fill = "white", colour = "black", stroke = 0.25) +
  geom_line(aes(y = pred, colour = model), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(data = cpue_gof_labels,
            aes(x = Inf, y = Inf, label = label, colour = model, vjust = vjust),
            hjust = 1.1, size = 2.6,
            inherit.aes = FALSE, show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ index, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, NA), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  scale_colour_opal(breaks = c("opal", "6-fishery opal", "SS3", "MFCL")) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Standardized CPUE", colour = "Model") +
  theme_opal()
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-bet-osa-residuals
#| fig-cap: "OSA residual diagnostics for the CPUE component of the fitted WCPO bigeye tuna diagnostic model, shown by index month. For this independent lognormal CPUE likelihood, the OSA residuals are equivalent to standardized log residuals; the flattened Q-Q pattern shows under-dispersion relative to the fitted CPUE observation variance."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 8

bet_osa <- bet$data$cpue_data %>%
  mutate(
    residual = bet$report$osa_cpue_residual,
    index = factor(month,
                   levels = sort(unique(month)),
                   labels = month.name[sort(unique(month))])
  )

bet_osa_qq <- bet_osa %>%
  group_by(index) %>%
  arrange(residual, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    q_theoretical = qnorm(ppoints(n())),
    panel = "Normal Q-Q"
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  transmute(index, panel, x = q_theoretical, y = residual)

bet_osa_time <- bet_osa %>%
  transmute(index, panel = "Residual by year", x = year, y = residual)

ggplot(bind_rows(bet_osa_qq, bet_osa_time), aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey55", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "grey55", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_abline(data = data.frame(panel = "Normal Q-Q"),
              aes(intercept = 0, slope = 1),
              inherit.aes = FALSE, colour = "grey55", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_segment(data = bet_osa_time,
               aes(x = x, xend = x, y = 0, yend = y),
               inherit.aes = FALSE, colour = "grey65", linewidth = 0.25) +
  geom_point(size = 0.9, alpha = 0.75, colour = "black") +
  facet_grid(index ~ panel, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "OSA residual") +
  theme_opal()
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-bet-lf-diagnostics
#| fig-cap: "Observed, predicted, and simulated length-composition proportions for WCPO bigeye tuna fisheries 8-14, summed over all years using the variance-adjusted effective sample sizes. Each simulated line independently simulates the fitted row-level compositions and then pools those simulated counts over all years within fishery."
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 8

bet_lf_pred <- bet$report$lf_pred
names(bet_lf_pred) <- bet$data$lf_fishery_f
for (k in seq_along(bet_lf_pred)) {
  colnames(bet_lf_pred[[k]]) <- bet$data$len_mid
}

bet_lf_rows <- bet_prediction_rows(
  bet_lf_pred,
  bet$data$lf_fishery_f,
  bet$data$lf_fishery,
  bet$data$lf_n
)

set.seed(100912)
bet_lf_mean <- bind_rows(
  bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    as.matrix(bet$data$lf_obs_in),
    bet$data$len_mid,
    as.numeric(bet$data$lf_n),
    bet$data$lf_fishery
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = paste("Fishery", .group),
      length = bin,
      proportion,
      series = "Observed"
    ),
  bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    bet_lf_rows$pred,
    bet$data$len_mid,
    bet_lf_rows$n_eff,
    bet_lf_rows$fishery
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = paste("Fishery", .group),
      length = bin,
      proportion,
      series = "Predicted"
    )
)

bet_lf_sim_df <- bet_simulate_pooled_composition(
  bet_lf_rows$pred,
  bet$data$len_mid,
  bet_lf_rows$n_eff,
  bet_lf_rows$fishery,
  nsim = 20L
) %>%
  transmute(
    fishery = paste("Fishery", .group),
    length = bin,
    proportion,
    sim_id
  )

bet_fishery_levels <- paste("Fishery", sort(unique(bet$data$wcpo_bet_lf$fishery)))
bet_lf_mean <- bet_lf_mean %>%
  mutate(fishery = factor(fishery, levels = bet_fishery_levels))
bet_lf_sim_df <- bet_lf_sim_df %>%
  mutate(fishery = factor(fishery, levels = bet_fishery_levels))
bet_lf_total_neff <- tibble(
  fishery = paste("Fishery", bet$data$lf_fishery),
  effective_n = as.numeric(bet$data$lf_n)
) %>%
  group_by(fishery) %>%
  summarise(effective_n = sum(effective_n, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    fishery = factor(fishery, levels = bet_fishery_levels),
    label = sprintf("Total Neff = %.1f", effective_n)
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_col(data = filter(bet_lf_mean, series == "Observed"),
           aes(x = length, y = proportion), fill = opal_role["observed"], width = 1.8) +
  geom_line(data = bet_lf_sim_df,
            aes(x = length, y = proportion, group = sim_id),
            colour = opal_role["simulated"], alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_line(data = filter(bet_lf_mean, series == "Predicted"),
            aes(x = length, y = proportion), colour = opal_role["fitted"], linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(data = bet_lf_total_neff,
            aes(x = Inf, y = Inf, label = label),
            hjust = 1.05, vjust = 1.25, size = 2.5,
            inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ fishery, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Length (cm)", y = "Pooled proportion") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, NA), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_opal()
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-bet-wf-diagnostics
#| fig-cap: "Observed, predicted, and simulated weight-composition proportions for active WCPO bigeye tuna weight-composition fisheries, summed over all years using the variance-adjusted effective sample sizes. Each simulated line independently simulates the fitted row-level compositions and then pools those simulated counts over all years within fishery."
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 8

bet_wf_pred <- bet$report$wf_pred
names(bet_wf_pred) <- bet$data$wf_fishery_f
for (k in seq_along(bet_wf_pred)) {
  colnames(bet_wf_pred[[k]]) <- bet$data$wt_mid
}

bet_wf_rows <- bet_prediction_rows(
  bet_wf_pred,
  bet$data$wf_fishery_f,
  bet$data$wf_fishery,
  bet$data$wf_n
)

set.seed(101015)
bet_wf_mean <- bind_rows(
  bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    as.matrix(bet$data$wf_obs_in),
    bet$data$wt_mid,
    as.numeric(bet$data$wf_n),
    bet$data$wf_fishery
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = paste("Fishery", .group),
      weight = bin,
      proportion,
      series = "Observed"
    ),
  bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    bet_wf_rows$pred,
    bet$data$wt_mid,
    bet_wf_rows$n_eff,
    bet_wf_rows$fishery
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = paste("Fishery", .group),
      weight = bin,
      proportion,
      series = "Predicted"
    )
)

bet_wf_sim_df <- bet_simulate_pooled_composition(
  bet_wf_rows$pred,
  bet$data$wt_mid,
  bet_wf_rows$n_eff,
  bet_wf_rows$fishery,
  nsim = 20L
) %>%
  transmute(
    fishery = paste("Fishery", .group),
    weight = bin,
    proportion,
    sim_id
  )

bet_wf_fishery_levels <- paste("Fishery", sort(unique(bet$data$wf_fishery_f)))
bet_wf_mean <- bet_wf_mean %>%
  mutate(fishery = factor(fishery, levels = bet_wf_fishery_levels))
bet_wf_sim_df <- bet_wf_sim_df %>%
  mutate(fishery = factor(fishery, levels = bet_wf_fishery_levels))
bet_wf_total_neff <- tibble(
  fishery = paste("Fishery", bet$data$wf_fishery),
  effective_n = as.numeric(bet$data$wf_n)
) %>%
  group_by(fishery) %>%
  summarise(effective_n = sum(effective_n, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    fishery = factor(fishery, levels = bet_wf_fishery_levels),
    label = sprintf("Total Neff = %.1f", effective_n)
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_col(data = filter(bet_wf_mean, series == "Observed"),
           aes(x = weight, y = proportion), fill = opal_role["observed"], width = 1.8) +
  geom_line(data = bet_wf_sim_df,
            aes(x = weight, y = proportion, group = sim_id),
            colour = opal_role["simulated"], alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_line(data = filter(bet_wf_mean, series == "Predicted"),
            aes(x = weight, y = proportion), colour = opal_role["fitted"], linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(data = bet_wf_total_neff,
            aes(x = Inf, y = Inf, label = label),
            hjust = 1.05, vjust = 1.25, size = 2.5,
            inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ fishery, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Weight (kg)", y = "Pooled proportion") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, NA), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_opal()
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-bet-agg6-composition-diagnostics
#| fig-cap: "Observed, predicted, and simulated composition proportions for the matched six-fishery WCPO bigeye tuna aggregation sensitivity, summed over all years using the variance-adjusted effective sample sizes. Length-composition groups are shown for groups 1--4 and weight-composition groups for groups 5--6; facet labels identify the original BET fisheries pooled into each group. Each simulated line independently simulates the fitted row-level compositions and then pools those simulated counts over all years within group."
#| fig-height: 6.4
#| fig-width: 8

set.seed(6010)

bet_agg6_lf_rows <- bet_prediction_rows(
  bet_agg6_report$lf_pred,
  bet_agg6_data$lf_fishery_f,
  bet_agg6_data$lf_fishery,
  bet_agg6_data$lf_n
)
bet_agg6_wf_rows <- bet_prediction_rows(
  bet_agg6_report$wf_pred,
  bet_agg6_data$wf_fishery_f,
  bet_agg6_data$wf_fishery,
  bet_agg6_data$wf_n
)

bet_agg6_comp_mean <- bind_rows(
  bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    as.matrix(bet_agg6_data$lf_obs_in),
    bet_agg6_data$len_mid,
    as.numeric(bet_agg6_data$lf_n),
    bet_agg6_data$lf_fishery
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = .group,
      bin,
      proportion,
      series = "Observed",
      composition = "Length composition"
    ),
  bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    bet_agg6_lf_rows$pred,
    bet_agg6_data$len_mid,
    bet_agg6_lf_rows$n_eff,
    bet_agg6_lf_rows$fishery
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = .group,
      bin,
      proportion,
      series = "Predicted",
      composition = "Length composition"
    ),
  bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    as.matrix(bet_agg6_data$wf_obs_in),
    bet_agg6_data$wt_mid,
    as.numeric(bet_agg6_data$wf_n),
    bet_agg6_data$wf_fishery
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = .group,
      bin,
      proportion,
      series = "Observed",
      composition = "Weight composition"
    ),
  bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    bet_agg6_wf_rows$pred,
    bet_agg6_data$wt_mid,
    bet_agg6_wf_rows$n_eff,
    bet_agg6_wf_rows$fishery
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = .group,
      bin,
      proportion,
      series = "Predicted",
      composition = "Weight composition"
    )
) %>%
  left_join(bet_agg6_group_labels, by = "fishery") %>%
  mutate(
    panel = paste0(composition, "\n", group_label),
    panel = factor(panel, levels = unique(panel))
  )

bet_agg6_comp_sim <- bind_rows(
  bet_simulate_pooled_composition(
    bet_agg6_lf_rows$pred,
    bet_agg6_data$len_mid,
    bet_agg6_lf_rows$n_eff,
    bet_agg6_lf_rows$fishery,
    nsim = 20L
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = .group,
      bin,
      proportion,
      sim_id,
      composition = "Length composition"
    ),
  bet_simulate_pooled_composition(
    bet_agg6_wf_rows$pred,
    bet_agg6_data$wt_mid,
    bet_agg6_wf_rows$n_eff,
    bet_agg6_wf_rows$fishery,
    nsim = 20L
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      fishery = .group,
      bin,
      proportion,
      sim_id,
      composition = "Weight composition"
    )
) %>%
  left_join(bet_agg6_group_labels, by = "fishery") %>%
  mutate(
    panel = paste0(composition, "\n", group_label),
    panel = factor(panel, levels = levels(bet_agg6_comp_mean$panel))
  )

bet_agg6_total_neff <- bind_rows(
  tibble(fishery = bet_agg6_data$lf_fishery,
         effective_n = as.numeric(bet_agg6_data$lf_n),
         composition = "Length composition"),
  tibble(fishery = bet_agg6_data$wf_fishery,
         effective_n = as.numeric(bet_agg6_data$wf_n),
         composition = "Weight composition")
) %>%
  group_by(composition, fishery) %>%
  summarise(effective_n = sum(effective_n, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  left_join(bet_agg6_group_labels, by = "fishery") %>%
  mutate(
    panel = paste0(composition, "\n", group_label),
    panel = factor(panel, levels = levels(bet_agg6_comp_mean$panel)),
    label = sprintf("Total Neff = %.1f", effective_n)
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_col(data = filter(bet_agg6_comp_mean, series == "Observed"),
           aes(x = bin, y = proportion),
           fill = opal_role["observed"], width = 1.8) +
  geom_line(data = bet_agg6_comp_sim,
            aes(x = bin, y = proportion, group = sim_id),
            colour = opal_role["simulated"], alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.32) +
  geom_line(data = filter(bet_agg6_comp_mean, series == "Predicted"),
            aes(x = bin, y = proportion),
            colour = opal_role["fitted"], linewidth = 0.75) +
  geom_text(data = bet_agg6_total_neff,
            aes(x = Inf, y = Inf, label = label),
            hjust = 1.05, vjust = 1.25, size = 2.25,
            inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ panel, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Composition bin", y = "Pooled proportion") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, NA), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_opal(base_size = 8.5) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 6.5))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| include: false
#| label: prep-bet-year-composition

select_panel_years <- function(years, max_years) {
  years <- sort(unique(years))
  if (length(years) <= max_years) return(years)
  years[unique(round(seq(1, length(years), length.out = max_years)))]
}

simulate_year_composition <- function(pred_matrix, bins, n_eff, year, bin_col,
                                      nsim = 12L) {
  bet_simulate_pooled_composition(
    pred_matrix = pred_matrix,
    bins = bins,
    n_eff = n_eff,
    group = year,
    nsim = nsim
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      year = .group,
      !!bin_col := bin,
      proportion,
      sim_id
    )
}

make_year_comp <- function(kind, fishery, nsim = 12L) {
  if (kind == "lf") {
    pred_list <- bet$report$lf_pred
    fishery_f <- bet$data$lf_fishery_f
    n_eff_rows <- split(bet$data$lf_n, bet$data$lf_fishery)[[as.character(fishery)]]
    obs_matrix <- bet$data$lf_obs_in[bet$data$lf_fishery == fishery, , drop = FALSE]
    bins <- bet$data$len_mid
    obs <- bet$data$wcpo_bet_lf %>% filter(fishery == !!fishery)
    bin_col <- "length"
  } else {
    pred_list <- bet$report$wf_pred
    fishery_f <- bet$data$wf_fishery_f
    n_eff_rows <- split(bet$data$wf_n, bet$data$wf_fishery)[[as.character(fishery)]]
    obs_matrix <- bet$data$wf_obs_in[bet$data$wf_fishery == fishery, , drop = FALSE]
    bins <- bet$data$wt_mid
    obs <- bet$data$wcpo_bet_wf %>% filter(fishery == !!fishery)
    bin_col <- "weight"
  }

  i <- which(fishery_f == fishery)
  pred <- as.matrix(pred_list[[i]])
  obs_rows <- obs %>%
    distinct(year, month, ts) %>%
    arrange(ts, year, month)
  if (nrow(obs_rows) != nrow(pred)) {
    stop("Observed rows do not align with predicted composition rows")
  }
  if (length(n_eff_rows) != nrow(pred)) {
    stop("Effective N rows do not align with predicted composition rows")
  }
  obs_rows <- obs_rows %>%
    mutate(effective_n = as.numeric(n_eff_rows))

  pred_year <- bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    pred,
    bins,
    obs_rows$effective_n,
    obs_rows$year
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      year = .group,
      !!bin_col := bin,
      proportion
    )

  obs_year <- bet_weighted_comp_summary(
    as.matrix(obs_matrix),
    bins,
    obs_rows$effective_n,
    obs_rows$year
  ) %>%
    transmute(
      year = .group,
      !!bin_col := bin,
      proportion
    )

  effective_n <- obs_rows %>%
    group_by(year) %>%
    summarise(effective_n = sum(effective_n, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(label = sprintf("Neff = %.1f", effective_n))

  list(
    observed = obs_year,
    predicted = pred_year,
    simulated = simulate_year_composition(
      pred,
      bins,
      obs_rows$effective_n,
      obs_rows$year,
      bin_col,
      nsim = nsim
    ),
    effective_n = effective_n,
    bin_col = bin_col
  )
}
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-bet-lf12-year-composition
#| fig-cap: "Year-specific observed, predicted, and simulated length-composition proportions for selected years of WCPO bigeye tuna fishery 12. Unlike the pooled length-composition diagnostics in @fig-bet-lf-diagnostics, this plot does not aggregate over all years; each panel has its own fitted and simulated composition. Panel text gives the variance-adjusted effective N used by the likelihood for that year."
#| fig-height: 7.2
#| fig-width: 8

set.seed(12012)
bet_lf12_year <- make_year_comp("lf", 12, nsim = 12L)
bet_lf12_panel_years <- select_panel_years(bet_lf12_year$predicted$year, 16L)

ggplot() +
  geom_col(data = filter(bet_lf12_year$observed, year %in% bet_lf12_panel_years),
           aes(x = length, y = proportion), fill = opal_role["observed"], width = 1.8) +
  geom_line(data = filter(bet_lf12_year$simulated, year %in% bet_lf12_panel_years),
            aes(x = length, y = proportion, group = sim_id),
            colour = opal_role["simulated"], alpha = 0.6, linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_line(data = filter(bet_lf12_year$predicted, year %in% bet_lf12_panel_years),
            aes(x = length, y = proportion), colour = opal_role["fitted"], linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(data = filter(bet_lf12_year$effective_n, year %in% bet_lf12_panel_years),
            aes(x = Inf, y = Inf, label = label),
            inherit.aes = FALSE, hjust = 1.05, vjust = 1.3,
            size = 2.1, colour = "grey25") +
  facet_wrap(~ year, ncol = 4, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Length (cm)", y = "Proportion") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, NA), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_opal() +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 7),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 6))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-bet-wf15-year-composition
#| fig-cap: "Year-specific observed, predicted, and simulated weight-composition proportions for selected years of WCPO bigeye tuna fishery 15. Unlike the pooled weight-composition diagnostics in @fig-bet-wf-diagnostics, this plot does not aggregate over all years; each panel has its own fitted and simulated composition. Panel text gives the variance-adjusted effective N used by the likelihood for that year."
#| fig-height: 7.2
#| fig-width: 8

set.seed(15015)
bet_wf15_year <- make_year_comp("wf", 15, nsim = 12L)
bet_wf15_panel_years <- select_panel_years(bet_wf15_year$predicted$year, 20L)

ggplot() +
  geom_col(data = filter(bet_wf15_year$observed, year %in% bet_wf15_panel_years),
           aes(x = weight, y = proportion), fill = opal_role["observed"], width = 1.8) +
  geom_line(data = filter(bet_wf15_year$simulated, year %in% bet_wf15_panel_years),
            aes(x = weight, y = proportion, group = sim_id),
            colour = opal_role["simulated"], alpha = 0.6, linewidth = 0.28) +
  geom_line(data = filter(bet_wf15_year$predicted, year %in% bet_wf15_panel_years),
            aes(x = weight, y = proportion), colour = opal_role["fitted"], linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(data = filter(bet_wf15_year$effective_n, year %in% bet_wf15_panel_years),
            aes(x = Inf, y = Inf, label = label),
            inherit.aes = FALSE, hjust = 1.05, vjust = 1.3,
            size = 2.1, colour = "grey25") +
  facet_wrap(~ year, ncol = 4, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Weight (kg)", y = "Proportion") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, NA), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_opal() +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 7),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 6))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| include: false
#| label: prep-bet-composition-residuals

bet_lf_residuals <- bet$diagnostics$lf_residuals %>%
  filter(is.finite(residual)) %>%
  transmute(
    type,
    Fishery,
    year,
    bin,
    residual,
    sign
  )

bet_wf_residuals <- bet$diagnostics$wf_residuals %>%
  filter(is.finite(residual)) %>%
  transmute(
    type,
    Fishery,
    year,
    bin,
    residual,
    sign
  )

bet_comp_residuals <- bind_rows(bet_lf_residuals, bet_wf_residuals) %>%
  mutate(
    type = factor(type, levels = c("Length composition", "Weight composition")),
    Fishery = factor(Fishery, levels = paste("Fishery", sort(unique(as.integer(sub("Fishery ", "", Fishery))))))
  )

plot_bet_comp_residuals <- function(plot_data, y_label) {
  plot_summary <- plot_data %>%
    group_by(Fishery, year, bin) %>%
```
